terms.definition: A decimal number equal to one hundredth, written with two digits after the decimal point.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 0.01 is the same as one hundredth or 1/100.
terms.common_errors: Confusing 0.01 with 0.1 — not understanding that more decimal places means a smaller value.

terms.definition: A fraction representing one part out of one hundred equal parts.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: One penny is 1/100 of a pound.
terms.common_errors: Thinking 1/100 is larger than 1/10 because 100 is larger than 10.

terms.definition: The product of twelve multiplied by twelve, equal to 144; the upper bound of times tables knowledge expected by the end of Year 4.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: By the end of Year 4, children should know all facts up to 12 × 12.

terms.definition: Relating to the operation of addition; an additive relationship involves combining or increasing quantities.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The additive inverse of 5 is -5 because 5 + (-5) = 0.
terms.common_errors: Confusing additive and multiplicative structures in word problems.

terms.definition: The amount of two-dimensional space enclosed within a boundary, measured in square units.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The area of this rectangle is 12 square centimetres.
terms.common_errors: Confusing area with perimeter — measuring around the shape instead of the space inside.

terms.definition: The plural of axis; the two reference lines (horizontal and vertical) on a coordinate grid or graph.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Label both axes on your graph before plotting the points.
terms.common_errors: Confusing the x-axis (horizontal) with the y-axis (vertical).

terms.definition: A value less than zero, indicating a negative number; commonly encountered in temperature contexts.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The temperature dropped to three degrees below zero overnight.
terms.common_errors: Thinking that -3 is greater than -1 because 3 is greater than 1.

terms.definition: An abbreviation for centimetre (cm) or a letter used as a variable to represent an unknown value in a formula.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In the formula for perimeter, c stands for the length of side c.
terms.common_errors: Confusing the letter c as a label (short for centimetre) with c as a variable.

terms.definition: A metric prefix meaning one hundredth (1/100), used in units of measurement.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There are 100 centimetres in a metre because centi- means one hundredth.
terms.common_errors: Confusing centi- (hundredth) with cent (hundred) — the prefix divides, not multiplies.

terms.definition: The unit of area equal to a square with sides of one centimetre; abbreviated as cm².
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: This shape covers 15 cm² on the grid paper.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting the squared symbol — writing cm instead of cm² when measuring area.

terms.definition: A written method for adding numbers by lining up the digits in place-value columns and working from right to left.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use column addition to find the total of 356 and 478.
terms.common_errors: Not aligning digits correctly in columns, leading to place-value errors.

terms.definition: A written method for subtracting numbers by lining up digits in place-value columns and exchanging where necessary.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use column subtraction to work out 503 minus 267.
terms.common_errors: Subtracting the smaller digit from the larger in each column instead of exchanging.

terms.definition: A value that appears in two or more sets, such as a number found in both a list of multiples of 3 and multiples of 4.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 12 is a common value in the multiples of 3 and the multiples of 4.

terms.definition: The rule that the order of numbers does not affect the result in addition and multiplication (e.g. 3 + 5 = 5 + 3).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Because of the commutative law, 7 × 4 gives the same answer as 4 × 7.
terms.common_errors: Applying commutativity to subtraction or division, where order does matter.

terms.definition: A concise form of recording a calculation, typically the standard column method without expanded working.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Now try the compact method for column multiplication.
terms.common_errors: Moving to compact methods before understanding the expanded form.

terms.definition: A fixed value that does not change, as opposed to a variable.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In the pattern 3, 6, 9, 12, the constant difference is 3.

terms.definition: Data that can take any value within a range, typically measured rather than counted (e.g. height, temperature).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Temperature is continuous data because it can be any value, not just whole numbers.
terms.common_errors: Confusing continuous data (measured) with discrete data (counted).

terms.definition: An ordered pair of numbers that describes a precise position on a grid, written as (x, y).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Plot the coordinate (3, 5) on the grid.
terms.common_errors: Reversing x and y values — going up before going across.

terms.definition: A method for finding area by counting the number of unit squares that fit inside a shape on a grid.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Count squares to find the area of this irregular shape.
terms.common_errors: Not counting partial squares or counting them as whole squares.

terms.definition: Continuing a counting sequence from positive numbers through zero into negative numbers, or vice versa.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Count backwards from 5 through zero: 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 0, -1, -2.
terms.common_errors: Skipping zero or being unsure what comes after 0 when counting backwards.

terms.definition: The decimal form of a fraction or percentage, showing the same value in a different notation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The decimal equivalent of 1/4 is 0.25.
terms.common_errors: Not recognising that 0.5 and 1/2 represent the same value.

terms.definition: The position of a digit after the decimal point, indicating its value (tenths, hundredths, etc.).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The number 3.47 has two decimal places.
terms.common_errors: Counting decimal places from the decimal point leftward instead of rightward.

terms.definition: The unit of temperature measurement used in the metric system, written as °C.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.etymology: Named after Anders Celsius, Swedish astronomer
terms.example_usage: Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius.

terms.definition: A calculation strategy that reaches the correct answer with the fewest steps or least effort.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: What is the most efficient method to add 99? Try adding 100 and subtracting 1.

terms.definition: A type of triangle where all three sides are equal in length and all three angles are 60°.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: An equilateral triangle has three sides that are all the same length.
terms.common_errors: Confusing equilateral (equal sides) with isosceles (two equal sides).

terms.definition: Two whole numbers that multiply together to give a particular product.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The factor pairs of 12 are 1 and 12, 2 and 6, 3 and 4.
terms.common_errors: Missing factor pairs, especially forgetting 1 × n as a factor pair.

terms.definition: A set of related calculations that use the same numbers, showing the inverse relationship between operations.
terms.subject: Mathematics

terms.definition: The top-right section of a coordinate grid where both x and y values are positive.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In Year 4, we plot coordinates in the first quadrant only.
terms.common_errors: Confusing which quadrant is the first one.

terms.definition: The ability to recall number facts and carry out procedures quickly, accurately, and with confidence.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Practise your times tables to build fluency in multiplication.

terms.definition: To bend a shape along a line to explore symmetry or to create equal parts.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Fold the paper in half to check if the shape is symmetrical.

terms.definition: A standard written algorithm for calculation (e.g. column addition, short multiplication) as distinct from mental or informal strategies.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use a formal method to multiply 236 by 7.
terms.common_errors: Applying the formal method without understanding the place value behind each step.

terms.definition: A mathematical rule expressed using letters and symbols that shows the relationship between quantities.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The formula for the area of a rectangle is length × width.
terms.common_errors: Confusing a formula with an equation — a formula defines a general rule.

terms.definition: A number with four digits, ranging from 1,000 to 9,999, spanning the thousands place.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 3,456 is a four-digit number.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting that 1,000 is the smallest four-digit number, not 999.

terms.definition: A network of horizontal and vertical lines forming squares, used for plotting coordinates, measuring area, or organising data.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Plot the shape on the coordinate grid.
terms.common_errors: Reading the grid lines instead of the spaces when counting squares for area.

terms.definition: The line running left to right along the bottom of a graph or coordinate grid, usually called the x-axis.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Label the horizontal axis with the months of the year.
terms.common_errors: Confusing horizontal (across) with vertical (up/down).

terms.definition: One of one hundred equal parts of a whole; the second digit after the decimal point represents hundredths.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In 0.35, the 5 is in the hundredths place.
terms.common_errors: Confusing hundredths with hundreds — one is very small, the other very large.

terms.definition: The plural form referring to multiple parts each equal to one hundredth of a whole.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 0.25 means 25 hundredths.
terms.common_errors: Reading 0.25 as twenty-five instead of twenty-five hundredths.

terms.definition: To estimate a value between two known data points on a graph by reading from the line.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use the line graph to interpolate the temperature at 2:30 pm.
terms.common_errors: Confusing interpolation (between known points) with extrapolation (beyond known points).

terms.definition: A type of triangle with exactly two sides of equal length and two equal angles.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Draw an isosceles triangle where two sides are 5 cm and one side is 3 cm.
terms.common_errors: Thinking isosceles triangles must always look like an arrow pointing up — they can be oriented any way.

terms.definition: A metric prefix meaning one thousand, used in units of measurement.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There are 1,000 grams in a kilogram because kilo- means one thousand.
terms.common_errors: Confusing kilo- (thousand) with centi- (hundredth) or milli- (thousandth).

terms.definition: The abbreviation for litres, a unit for measuring the capacity or volume of liquids.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: This bottle holds 2 l of water.
terms.common_errors: Confusing the letter l with the number 1.

terms.definition: A graph that uses points connected by lines to show how data changes over time or another continuous variable.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Draw a line graph to show the temperature each hour.
terms.common_errors: Joining points with curves instead of straight lines, or using line graphs for non-continuous data.

terms.definition: A calculation performed in your head without writing down intermediate steps, using number facts and known strategies.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use mental calculation to work out 48 + 25.
terms.related_everyday_word: working it out in your head

terms.definition: The exact middle point between two positions, values, or coordinates.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: What is the midpoint between 0 and 10 on the number line?

terms.definition: A metric prefix meaning one thousandth (1/1000), used in units of measurement.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There are 1,000 millilitres in a litre because milli- means one thousandth.
terms.common_errors: Confusing milli- (thousandth) with centi- (hundredth).

terms.definition: The numbers produced by multiplying 6 by whole numbers: 6, 12, 18, 24, 30 and so on.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: List the first five multiples of six: 6, 12, 18, 24, 30.
terms.common_errors: Including 0 or starting from 1 instead of 6.

terms.definition: The unit of area equal to a square with sides of one metre; abbreviated as m².
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The classroom floor is about 50 m².
terms.common_errors: Confusing m (length in metres) with m² (area in square metres).

terms.definition: A number less than zero, shown with a minus sign in front of it.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The temperature today is negative two degrees.
terms.common_errors: Thinking negative numbers get smaller as the digit increases (e.g. -5 is less than -2, not more).

terms.definition: A single part when a whole is divided into one hundred equal parts; equivalent to 0.01.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: One penny is one hundredth of a pound.
terms.common_errors: Confusing one hundredth (0.01) with one tenth (0.1).

terms.definition: Two numbers written in a specific order within brackets to describe a position on a coordinate grid, always (x, y).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The ordered pair (4, 7) means go 4 across and 7 up.
terms.common_errors: Reversing the order — writing (y, x) instead of (x, y).

terms.definition: The direction or angle at which a shape is positioned; a shape remains the same regardless of how it is turned.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A square is still a square even if you change its orientation.
terms.common_errors: Thinking a rotated shape is a different shape — e.g. a rotated square is still a square, not a diamond.

terms.definition: The point where the x-axis and y-axis cross on a coordinate grid, with coordinates (0, 0).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Start at the origin (0, 0) and go 3 along the x-axis.

terms.definition: A four-sided shape (quadrilateral) where both pairs of opposite sides are parallel and equal in length.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A rectangle is a special type of parallelogram where all angles are right angles.
terms.common_errors: Not recognising rectangles and squares as types of parallelogram.

terms.definition: To mark a point on a graph or grid at a specified position using coordinates.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Plot the point (3, 5) on the coordinate grid.
terms.common_errors: Reading along the wrong axis first when plotting coordinates.

terms.definition: The mathematical characteristics of a shape or number, such as the number of sides, angles, or factors.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Describe the properties of a rectangle: four sides, four right angles, opposite sides equal.

terms.definition: A shape made entirely of straight lines that meet at right angles, like an L-shape or T-shape.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Find the area of this rectilinear shape by splitting it into rectangles.
terms.common_errors: Confusing rectilinear with rectangular — rectilinear shapes have right angles but are not necessarily rectangles.

terms.definition: To flip a shape over a mirror line to create a mirror image of the original.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Reflect the triangle in the vertical mirror line.
terms.common_errors: Moving the shape instead of flipping it, or not keeping each point the same distance from the mirror line.

terms.definition: The mirror image of a shape produced by flipping it over a line of symmetry.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Draw the reflection of this shape in the mirror line.
terms.common_errors: Rotating the shape instead of reflecting it.

terms.definition: To rearrange numbers or operations into a different sequence, often to make a calculation easier.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Reorder 3 + 47 as 47 + 3 to make the addition easier.

terms.definition: A four-sided shape (quadrilateral) where all four sides are equal in length; a tilted square.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A rhombus has four equal sides but its angles are not necessarily right angles.
terms.common_errors: Thinking a rhombus must be tilted — a square is actually a special rhombus.

terms.definition: Containing an angle of exactly 90 degrees.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A right-angled triangle has one angle that is exactly 90°.
terms.common_errors: Thinking right-angled means the triangle points to the right.

terms.definition: To reduce a number to a lower value when rounding, because the deciding digit is less than 5.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: When rounding 342 to the nearest hundred, round down to 300.
terms.common_errors: Rounding down when the digit is exactly 5 — by convention, 5 rounds up.

terms.definition: To increase a number to a higher value when rounding, because the deciding digit is 5 or more.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: When rounding 367 to the nearest hundred, round up to 400.

terms.definition: A type of triangle where all three sides are different lengths and all three angles are different.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Draw a scalene triangle with sides of 3 cm, 4 cm, and 5 cm.
terms.common_errors: Confusing scalene with isosceles — scalene has no equal sides at all.

terms.definition: A compact written method for multiplying a multi-digit number by a single digit, working column by column.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use short multiplication to calculate 346 × 7.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting to carry over when a column product exceeds 9.

terms.definition: A unit of area equal to a square measuring 1 cm by 1 cm, written as cm².
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The area of this shape is 24 square centimetres.
terms.common_errors: Confusing square centimetres (area) with centimetres (length).

terms.definition: A unit of area equal to a square measuring 1 m by 1 m, written as m².
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The school hall has an area of about 200 square metres.
terms.common_errors: Thinking 1 m² equals 100 cm² instead of 10,000 cm².

terms.definition: Relating to the operation of subtraction; a subtractive relationship involves taking away or finding the difference.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Roman numerals use a subtractive principle: IV means 5 minus 1.

terms.definition: A measure of how hot or cold something is, read from a thermometer and expressed in degrees.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Read the temperature on the thermometer — it shows 15 degrees Celsius.
terms.common_errors: Difficulty reading scales where not every line is labelled.

terms.definition: Parts of a whole divided into ten equal pieces; the first digit after the decimal point represents tenths.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In the number 3.7, the 7 represents 7 tenths.
terms.common_errors: Confusing tenths with tens — one is a fraction, the other a whole-number place.

terms.definition: The place-value column representing groups of one thousand (1,000); the fourth digit from the right.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In 5,432 the digit 5 is in the thousands column.
terms.common_errors: Confusing thousands with hundredths due to similar-sounding names.

terms.definition: A line graph where the horizontal axis represents time, showing how a measured quantity changes over a period.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Draw a time graph to show the height of the plant each week.
terms.common_errors: Not spacing time intervals evenly along the horizontal axis.

terms.definition: A four-sided shape (quadrilateral) with exactly one pair of parallel sides.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A trapezium has one pair of parallel sides and one pair that are not parallel.
terms.common_errors: Confusing trapezium with parallelogram — a parallelogram has two pairs of parallel sides.

terms.definition: The general direction or pattern shown in a graph — whether values are going up, going down, or staying the same.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The line graph shows an upward trend in temperature during the morning.
terms.common_errors: Focusing on individual data points instead of the overall direction.

terms.definition: The number 25; in the curriculum context, a key benchmark number for understanding quarters and percentages.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 25 is one quarter of 100, so 25% means 25 out of 100.

terms.definition: The line running up and down on the left side of a graph or coordinate grid, usually called the y-axis.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Label the vertical axis with the number of children.
terms.common_errors: Confusing vertical (up/down) with horizontal (left/right).

terms.definition: The measurement of how wide something is, typically the shorter horizontal dimension of a shape.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The width of the rectangle is 4 cm and the length is 7 cm.
terms.common_errors: Confusing width with length — width is usually the shorter side.

terms.definition: The first number in an ordered pair, telling you how far to move horizontally from the origin.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In the coordinate (3, 5), the x-coordinate is 3 — go 3 along.
terms.common_errors: Confusing x-coordinate (horizontal) with y-coordinate (vertical).

terms.definition: The second number in an ordered pair, telling you how far to move vertically from the origin.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In the coordinate (3, 5), the y-coordinate is 5 — go 5 up.
terms.common_errors: Reading the y-coordinate first instead of second.
